title: 10 best herbal teas for upset stomach
intro: For an upset stomach, herbal teas can provide great relief. Peppermint tea is one of the best options due to its calming and antispasmodic properties that help soothe the stomach and improve digestion. Ginger tea is another excellent choice, as it is well-known for its anti-inflammatory and nausea-reducing qualities. Chamomile tea, with its soothing and gentle sedative effect, can help relax the stomach muscles and reduce stomach inflammation. Lastly, licorice root tea may help increase mucus production in the stomach lining, providing a protective barrier against stomach acid. Before starting any herbal tea remedy, it's best to consult with a healthcare professional to ensure it's safe and suitable for your specific needs.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea contains anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which soothe the stomach lining and reduce inflammation. The calming effects of chamomile tea's volatile oils target the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract, helping to relax stomach muscles and alleviate discomfort. Furthermore, chamomile's daisy-like flowers possess flavonoids that stimulate the production of gastric juices, promoting healthy digestion and aiding in the prevention of acid reflux. Chamomile tea's mild sedative properties can also help reduce stress and anxiety, indirectly contributing to overall digestive well-being by diminishing the negative impacts of these factors on the gastrointestinal system. Lastly, chamomile tea's tannins act as astringents, helping to protect the mucous membrane of the stomach and intestines, and reducing the risk of irritation and inflammation.
tea_parts: Flowers: Chamomile flowers are used to make medicinal tea for upset stomach due to their calming properties, which can help reduce inflammation and relax the muscles of the digestive tract.
Leaves: Chamomile leaves have anti-spasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties that can soothe the digestive system, providing relief for upset stomach.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds contain therapeutic compounds that can help alleviate symptoms of upset stomach, including gas, bloating, and indigestion.
tea_recipe: Boil 8 ounces of water in a teapot or kettle for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers to a teapot or infuser.
Pour the hot water over the chamomile flowers and steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the spent flowers.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day, for stomach upset relief.
tea_constituents: Apigenin: A flavonoid that has anti-spasmodic properties, helping to reduce smooth muscle spasms in the stomach.
Bisabolol: A terpene that has anti-inflammatory properties, helping to decrease inflammation in the stomach lining.
Chamazulene: A chemical compound that has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, helping to protect the stomach lining from damage.
Matricin: A precursor to chamazulene, matricin also has anti-inflammatory properties, aiding in the relief of stomach inflammation.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger, a key ingredient in ginger tea, contains gingerols and shogaols, compounds with proven anti-inflammatory and digestive properties. These compounds work by blocking nerve signals that cause nausea, reducing inflammation in the digestive tract, and relaxing intestinal muscles to alleviate stomach discomfort. Additionally, ginger increases saliva and bile production, which aids in the digestion of food and movement of food through the gastrointestinal tract. Ginger tea, when consumed during or after meals, can effectively soothe upset stomachs by enhancing digestion and providing rapid relief from symptoms such as cramps, bloating, and indigestion. Thus, ginger tea's ability to help with upset stomachs is a result of its active components' ability to influence various aspects of the digestive process.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: Ginger rhizomes are a popular choice for medicinal tea due to their anti-inflammatory properties that aid in soothing digestive discomfort.
Roots: Ginger roots contain bioactive compounds that alleviate nausea, thus making them ideal for upset stomach relief.
Leaves: Rich in antioxidants, ginger leaves help treat gastrointestinal irritations, making them useful in calming an upset stomach.
tea_recipe: Peel and chop 1 inch of fresh ginger into small pieces, then place in a tea infuser or loose in a teapot.
Boil 4 cups of water and pour over the ginger, letting it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the ginger pieces from the teapot.
Add honey and lemon juice to taste, stirring until fully dissolved.
Serve the ginger tea while warm, sipping slowly to soothe the stomach.
tea_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols are the main medicinal constituents in ginger, known for their anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which help alleviate upset stomach and soothe digestion.
Shogaols: Shogaols, derived from gingerols, further aid in reducing inflammation and nausea, thereby providing relief from upset stomach.
Paradols: Paradols, a product of gingerol degradation, exhibit antiemetic properties, helping to relieve symptoms of upset stomach such as nausea and vomiting.
Gingerdiols: Gingerdiols contribute to ginger's overall effectiveness in treating upset stomach by providing additional antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory benefits.

teas.tea_desc: Peppermint tea provides relief for upset stomachs due to its antispasmodic properties, which relax the gastrointestinal muscles, enabling smoother digestion. This herbal tea contains menthol, a compound that soothes the stomach lining, reducing inflammation and providing instant comfort. Moreover, peppermint tea stimulates the production of bile, thus accelerating the digestion process and preventing the buildup of gas and bloating. Furthermore, its calming aroma and mild flavor make it an ideal beverage for those experiencing nausea, helping to alleviate symptoms quickly. Therefore, peppermint tea is an effective and natural remedy for settling an upset stomach.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the peppermint plant contain menthol, a known muscle relaxant that soothes the intestinal tract, providing relief for upset stomach.
Stems: Peppermint stems carry essential oils that have antispasmodic properties, which helps to reduce pain and discomfort caused by upset stomach.
Flowers: Peppermint flowers have calming effects on the digestive system, making them ideal for treating upset stomach and associated symptoms.
Seeds: Peppermint seeds contain thymol, which helps to relax gastrointestinal muscles, making them useful for alleviating upset stomach.
tea_recipe: In a small pot, boil 8 ounces of water for 3-5 minutes.
Crush or tear 10 fresh peppermint leaves, add to the pot and let it steep for 5 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the leaves.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day, for stomach relaxation.
tea_constituents: Menthol: Menthol has antispasmodic properties that help relieve smooth muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing symptoms of upset stomach and associated symptoms like bloating and gas.
Menthone: Menthone also has antispasmodic properties and can help with digestion by increasing the production of bile and gastric juices.
Limonene: Limonene is known for its calming and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation in the stomach and soothe digestive issues.
Eugenol: Eugenol has antimicrobial and analgesic properties that help with stomach pain and inflammation, and can help eliminate harmful bacteria in the stomach that can cause digestive issues.

teas.tea_desc: Fennel tea aids digestion by stimulating the production of gastric juices, shortening digestion time, and facilitating the movement of food through the digestive tract. Its carminative properties reduce bloating and gas, providing relief from uncomfortable stomach distension. Fennel's anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic agents soothe the gastrointestinal lining, alleviating irritation and discomfort. Furthermore, fennel tea's antibacterial qualities combat harmful bacteria in the stomach, promoting a healthy gut environment and preventing further stomach issues. Consequently, fennel tea is an effective natural remedy for upset stomachs.
tea_parts: Seeds: Fennel seeds contain volatile oils that stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, providing quick relief for upset stomach.
Leaves: The leaves of fennel have carminative properties that help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing discomfort and pain.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of fennel are rich in antioxidants that protect the stomach lining, helping to prevent and soothe upset stomach.
tea_recipe: Chop: Slice 1 tablespoon of fresh fennel seeds or 1 teaspoon of crushed fennel seeds.
Steep: Place the chopped fennel in a tea infuser or loose in a teapot, then pour 1 cup of boiling water over it.
Cover: Cover and let the tea steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain: Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the fennel solids.
Serve: Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day, as needed for stomach discomfort.
tea_constituents: Anethole: This compound has antispasmodic properties, which can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and provide relief from upset stomach.
Fenchone: Fenchone has carminative effects, which means it can help reduce bloating and gas in the digestive tract.
Dianethole: This constituent has antimicrobial properties, which can help eliminate harmful bacteria in the stomach that may cause upset stomach.
Methyl chavicol: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and provide relief from symptoms of upset stomach.
Limonene: Limonene has been shown to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, which can help improve digestion and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach.
Estragole: This compound has antioxidant properties, which can help protect the digestive tract from damage caused by free radicals and reduce the risk of stomach ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea combats upset stomach by soothing inflammation in the digestive tract, as it contains substances like glycyrrhizic acid. This acid generates anti-inflammatory and mucosal protective effects, providing relief to the stomach lining. Moreover, licorice tea encourages the production of mucus in the gastrointestinal tract, forming a protective barrier against acidic environments. Additionally, it promotes the secretion of digestive enzymes, enhancing the breakdown of food and reducing stomach discomfort. Lastly, licorice tea addresses upset stomach by stimulating the regeneration of stomach cells, contributing to overall gut health and quicker recovery.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in glycyrrhizin, a compound with anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe irritated stomach lining.
Rhizomes: Packed with flavonoids and coumarins, they aid in digestion, reducing discomfort and upset stomach.
Leaves: Contain various beneficial compounds, including flavonoids, that help reduce stomach inflammation and provide relief from upset stomach.
Seeds: Loaded with antioxidants like phenolic acids, seeds help alleviate digestive issues and ease symptoms of upset stomach.
tea_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and add 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root, simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Turn off heat, cover and let the tea steep for 5 more minutes.
Strain the tea to remove licorice root pieces.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your stomach.
tea_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound is the primary active component in licorice root and has been shown to have anti-inflammatory effects in the gastrointestinal tract, which can help reduce stomach irritation and inflammation.
Flavonoids: These compounds have antispasmodic properties, which can help relax the smooth muscles in the gastrointestinal tract and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach, such as cramping and pain.
Coumarins: These compounds have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, which can help reduce pain and inflammation in the stomach.
Triterpenoids: These compounds have been shown to have gastroprotective effects, which can help prevent and heal stomach ulcers and other gastrointestinal injuries.
Volatile oils: These compounds have carminative properties, which can help reduce bloating, gas, and other symptoms of upset stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Turmeric tea combats upset stomach due to curcumin, its active ingredient, which possesses anti-inflammatory properties. This substance inhibits the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like compounds responsible for causing inflammation and resulting stomach pain. Furthermore, curcumin stimulates the release of bile, aiding in digestion and alleviating symptoms of indigestion and bloating. The soothing warmth of the tea increases blood flow to the abdominal area and promotes muscle relaxation, thereby further reducing discomfort. Finally, turmeric's antioxidant characteristics protect the stomach lining from damage, offering a comprehensive approach to managing digestive issues.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: These are underground stem structures that are commonly used to make medicinal tea for upset stomach due to their anti-inflammatory and carminative properties.
Roots: The roots of the turmeric plant contain curcumin, a compound known for its potent antioxidant and gastroprotective effects, which aids in treating upset stomach.

Leaves: The leaves have been used in traditional medicine to treat digestive issues, including upset stomach, due to their richness in therapeutic compounds and soothing effects on the gastrointestinal tract.
Flowers: While not as commonly used as the rhizomes or leaves, turmeric flowers possess anti-inflammatory and digestive properties, making them suitable for treating upset stomach.
tea_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 4 cups of water and 1 teaspoon of ground turmeric; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to a simmer and let the mixture steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a teapot or mug.
Stir in honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Serve warm and sip slowly.
tea_constituents: Curcumin: This is the main active ingredient in turmeric. It has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce irritation and promote stomach lining health.
Volatile oils: These include Turmerone, Atlantone, and Zingiberene. They can help stimulate the production of bile by the gallbladder, which may improve digestion and reduce stomach discomfort.

Curcuminoids: A group of compounds that include curcumin, demethoxycurcumin, and bisdemethoxycurcumin. They have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help relieve stomach cramps and spasms.
Potassium: This essential mineral can help regulate fluid balance in the body and promote healthy digestion, reducing the risk of upset stomach.
Dietary fiber: Turmeric contains a small amount of dietary fiber, which can help promote regular bowel movements, alleviate constipation, and maintain a healthy gut.
Vitamin C: Turmeric contains a small amount of vitamin C, an antioxidant that can help reduce oxidative stress in the gut and promote overall digestive health.

teas.tea_desc: Slippery elm tea provides relief for upset stomachs due to its antioxidant and demulcent properties. This tea contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel coats the lining of the stomach and digestive tract, protecting it from acid and irritation. Slippery elm also stimulates the production of natural mucus in the stomach, providing further protection and soothing effects. Moreover, it encourages the healing of any inflammation or damage in the stomach lining, while its antioxidant compounds help fight off free radicals that can cause harm to cells.
tea_parts: Bark: The bark of the slippery elm plant contains a gel-like substance that coats the stomach lining, providing relief from upset stomach.
Leaves: The leaves of the slippery elm plant contain mucilage, which soothes inflammation and irritation in the digestive tract, alleviating upset stomach.
Inner Bark: The inner bark of the slippery elm plant is rich in antioxidants and provides a demulcent effect, protecting the stomach lining and reducing symptoms of upset stomach.
Seeds: The seeds of the slippery elm plant contain mucilage, which helps to create a protective barrier in the stomach, reducing discomfort and irritation.
Roots: The roots of the slippery elm plant have demulcent properties, forming a soothing film over the mucous membranes, providing relief from upset stomach symptoms.
tea_recipe: Measure - Precisely measure out 1-2 teaspoons of dried slippery elm bark and place it in a tea infuser or loose in a teapot.
Boil - Bring 8 ounces of water to a boil, then immediately pour it over the slippery elm bark.
Steep - Allow the tea to steep, covered, for 5-10 minutes to extract the beneficial properties.
Strain - Strain the tea to remove the bark fragments before pouring the liquid into a cup.
Serve - Drink the slippery elm tea while it is still warm, up to three times a day as needed to soothe an upset stomach.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The gel-like substance forming a soothing coating on the stomach lining, protecting it from irritation and inflammation.
Tannins: These astringent compounds help to reduce inflammation and heal wounds in the digestive tract, providing relief from stomach discomfort.
Flavonoids: Antioxidant compounds with anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce irritation and promote healing in the gastrointestinal tract.
Starch: A complex carbohydrate that can help absorb excess stomach acid, reducing irritation and discomfort in the digestive system.
Gallic acid: A phenolic acid with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, helping to soothe and protect the stomach lining from irritation.
Kaempferol: A flavonoid that has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, aiding in reducing inflammation in the digestive tract and promoting healing.
Vitamin C: An essential nutrient that supports the immune system, promotes healing, and can help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by inflammation and irritation.

teas.tea_desc: Marshmallow tea, derived from the marshmallow plant's root, contains mucilage, a substance known for its soothing properties. This mucilage forms a protective layer on the stomach lining, reducing irritation. As a result, marshmallow tea alleviates discomfort and decreases painful stomach inflammation. Moreover, it helps control excessive stomach acid, which often leads to acid reflux and heartburn. Additionally, this tea's calming effect on the digestive tract promotes intestinal movement, thus relieving bloating and constipation.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, which soothes and protects the stomach lining, providing relief from upset stomach.
Flowers: The flowers of the marshmallow plant are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate digestive discomforts.
Roots: The roots of the marshmallow plant contain high amounts of mucilage, making it an effective remedy for soothing an upset stomach and reducing inflammation.

Leaves and Flowers: A mix of marshmallow leaves and flowers produces a tea with a high mucilage content, forming a protective layer on the stomach lining, reducing irritation, and promoting faster recovery.
tea_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water, 1 tablespoon of freshly grated ginger, and 1 cinnamon stick; bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 10 minutes.
Add 2 marshmallows (preferably homemade or natural) to the pot, stirring until melted and fully integrated.
Remove from heat, let cool for 3-5 minutes, then strain the tea to remove solids.
Stir in 1-2 teaspoons of honey (adjust to taste) and a squeeze of fresh lemon juice.
Serve immediately, sipping slowly to soothe the stomach.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow tea coats the stomach lining, protecting it from irritants and soothing digestive discomfort.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids found in marshmallow tea have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating upset stomach symptoms.
Polysaccharides: Polysaccharides in marshmallow tea act as a demulcent, forming a soothing film over the mucous membranes of the digestive tract, providing relief from stomach discomfort.
Ascorbic Acid: Vitamin C in marshmallow tea supports overall immune function, helping the body combat any potential infections that may contribute to upset stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Catnip tea contains nepetalactone, an essential oil known for its anti-spasmodic and calming properties. This oil helps soothe the smooth muscle linings of the digestive tract, reducing intestinal spasms and gas that cause upset stomachs. Moreover, catnip acts as a mild sedative and nervine, alleviating stress and anxiety that often accompany digestive issues. Its carminative properties facilitate the expulsion of excess gas, while its astringent qualities help tone the gastrointestinal system. As a result, catnip tea brings quick relief to an upset stomach by directly addressing multiple causes of discomfort.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in medicinal compounds, leaves are ideal for soothing an upset stomach.
Flowerings: Packed with antioxidants, flowerings ease stomach discomfort and promote digestive health.
Stems: Containing anti-inflammatory properties, stems offer relief to an irritated stomach.
Buds: Abundant in soothing agents, buds help calm the digestive system during upset.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried catnip leaves with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture, covered, for 10 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse.
Strain the tea to remove the catnip leaves, preventing bitterness in the final drink.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste for improved flavor.
Drink the catnip tea warm, up to three times a day, for soothing relief of an upset stomach.
tea_constituents: Nepetalactone: This compound is responsible for the calming effects of catnip and may help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation, which can be beneficial for an upset stomach.
Valepotriates: These iridoid glycosides have antispasmodic and carminative properties, meaning they can help relax the gastrointestinal tract and reduce gas and bloating.
Nepetalic acid: This organic acid has anti-inflammatory effects, which can help soothe an irritated stomach lining and reduce inflammation.
Tannins: These astringent polyphenolic biomolecules can help protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation, making them useful for treating digestive issues.

Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic acid has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach.
Flavonoids: These polyphenolic compounds have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the digestive tract and promote healthy digestion.

teas.tea_desc: Lemongrass tea brings immediate relief to an upset stomach due to its carminative properties, which move gas from the intestinal tract and alleviate symptoms of bloating and discomfort. This tea's antimicrobial qualities, derived from its citral and geraniol components, combat harmful bacteria in the stomach contributing to indigestion. Furthermore, lemongrass tea's soothing nature reduces inflammation in the digestive tract, calming any irritation and promoting healing. Additionally, its rich antioxidant profile combats free radicals, supporting overall gastrointestinal health and aiding in the prevention of future stomach upsets. Ultimately, the relaxing effects of lemongrass tea, which can be attributed to its essential oil content, settle the stomach and help restore normal digestive function.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of lemongrass are rich in antioxidants and volatile oils, providing soothing relief for upset stomachs.
Rhizomes: Lemongrass rhizomes contain beneficial compounds, such as citral and limonene, known to alleviate digestive discomfort.
Stems: The hollow stems of lemongrass hold essential oils and provide calming effects on the stomach, making them ideal for medicinal tea.
tea_recipe: Peel and crush 2 stalks of lemongrass to release their oils, taking 2 minutes.
In a pot, combine the crushed lemongrass with 4 cups of water and bring to a boil, taking 10 minutes.
Reduce heat and let the lemongrass simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the lemongrass pieces, then let it cool to a drinkable temperature.
Sip the tea slowly to soothe your upset stomach, enjoying its lemony flavor and aroma.
tea_constituents: Citral: This compound has antispasmodic properties, helping to relax the smooth muscles in the gastrointestinal tract and alleviating symptoms of upset stomach.
Geraniol: With antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, geraniol helps maintain a healthy gut environment and combats factors that may contribute to upset stomach.
Neral: Similar to citral, neral also exhibits antispasmodic effects, helping to soothe the digestive system and reduce symptoms of an upset stomach, such as cramping and bloating.
Citronellal: As an antifoaming agent, citronellal helps prevent the formation of excessive gas in the gastrointestinal tract, alleviating feelings of bloating and discomfort.

teas.tea_desc: Holy basil tea contains compounds that respond to digestive distress, such as eugenol and rosmarinic acid, which have documented anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. These components help decrease inflammation in the stomach lining, soothing discomfort and irritation. Moreover, holy basil tea stimulates the stomach's mucus production, providing a protective barrier against acidity. This herbal tea also promotes gastric juice secretion, enhancing digestion and preventing symptoms related to indigestion. Finally, its carminative properties help expel intestinal gas, alleviating bloating and discomfort associated with an upset stomach.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in anti-oxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, leaves of holy basil ease stomach discomfort.
Flowers: Packed with essential oils, flowers of holy basil soothe digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach.
Seeds: Abundant in anti-spasmodic properties, seeds of holy basil provide relief from stomach cramps and spasms.

teas.tea_desc: Dandelion tea promotes digestion due to its bitter properties, stimulating the release of digestive juices and bile from the liver. This tea also boasts anti-inflammatory properties, which can reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, soothing discomfort. Furthermore, dandelion tea's richness in antioxidants helps protect the gastrointestinal tract from damage caused by oxidative stress. Its mild diuretic effect aids in eliminating waste and toxins, easing the burden on the digestive system. Lastly, dandelion tea can help restore balance to the gut's bacterial flora, contributing to a healthy digestive process and alleviating upset stomach symptoms.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in nutrients, the roots of dandelions have been known to soothe digestive discomforts.
Leaves: The bitter leaves of dandelions stimulate digestive juices, easing symptoms of upset stomach.
Flowers: High in antioxidants, dandelion flowers offer relief from digestive ailments while providing liver support.

teas.tea_desc: Milk thistle tea brings relief to upset stomachs due to its powerful active compounds, particularly silymarin, which boast anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. These properties work together to protect the stomach lining, reduce irritation, and promote repair of damaged tissues. Moreover, milk thistle tea stimulates the production of bile, aiding digestion and the breakdown of fats, further soothing the digestive tract. Additionally, its antispasmodic effects help relax the smooth muscles of the intestines, alleviating discomfort and preventing spasms that cause cramps and pain. Ultimately, milk thistle tea's ability to restore normal gut function and maintain a healthy gastrointestinal tract ensures effective relief from upset stomachs.
tea_parts: Seeds: The seeds of milk thistle contain silymarin, a flavonoid that aids in digestion and soothes upset stomachs.
Leaves: Milk thistle leaves have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation in the stomach, providing relief from upset stomachs.
Flowers: Milk thistle flowers are rich in antioxidants that protect the stomach lining and promote healthy digestion, alleviating upset stomachs.

teas.tea_desc: Rose hip tea eases upset stomachs due to its anti-inflammatory properties, which reduce irritation in the stomach lining. This herbal tea, rich in vitamin C, aids in the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that regulate muscle contractions in the gut, promoting a smooth digestive process. Moreover, rose hip tea contains antioxidants that help in healing damaged stomach tissue, further alleviating discomfort. Its high fiber content also adds bulk to stools, preventing constipation and relieving an upset stomach. Lastly, rose hip tea's tannins act as astringents, reducing inflammation and helping to restore the stomach's normal function.
tea_parts: Seeds: Rich in vitamin C, seeds of rose hips ease upset stomach by reducing inflammation in the digestive tract.
Rhyzomes: Containing anti-inflammatory properties, rhyzomes of rose hips soothe irritation in the stomach lining.
Fruits: High in antioxidants, fruits of rose hips relieve symptoms of upset stomach by protecting the stomach from damage.
Leaves: Packed with tannins, leaves of rose hips alleviate stomach discomfort by reducing stomach acidity.
Flowers: Abundant in essential oils, flowers of rose hips calm digestive issues by promoting the production of digestive enzymes.
Bark: Source of phenolic compounds, bark of rose hips heals upset stomach by repairing damaged stomach tissue.

teas.tea_desc: Sage tea provides relief for upset stomachs due to its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, which help reduce inflammation and fight off harmful bacteria in the stomach. This herbal tea also contains rosmarinic acid, which aids in decreasing the production of inflammatory chemicals in the body. Furthermore, sage tea stimulates the digestive system, promoting the secretion of digestive juices and enzymes that help break down food and protect the stomach lining. Additionally, its calming effects on the gastrointestinal tract soothe stomach muscles, alleviating discomfort and promoting normal bowel movements.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in volatile oils and flavonoids, sage leaves ease stomach discomfort by promoting digestion and soothing intestinal inflammation.
Rhyzomes: High in essential oils and phenolic acids, sage rhyzomes alleviate stomach irritation and reduce symptoms of upset stomach, such as cramps and nausea.
Flowers: Packed with antioxidants and soothing compounds, sage flowers help calm the digestive system, addressing symptoms of upset stomach and supporting overall gastrointestinal health.

teas.tea_desc: Chrysanthemum tea, derived from the Chrysanthemum morifolium flower, offers reliable relief for upset stomachs due to its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. These properties stem from the high concentration of flavonoids and antioxidants present in the tea, which help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining and relax gastrointestinal muscles. Furthermore, chrysanthemum tea's gentle diuretic effect aids in flushing out toxins and excess fluids, thus alleviating discomfort and promoting overall digestive health. Its soothing nature also helps lower stress hormones, such as cortisol, that can exacerbate digestive issues. In summary, chrysanthemum tea's anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, diuretic, and stress-reducing properties combine to deliver an effective and natural solution for upset stomachs.
tea_parts: Flowers: Chrysanthemum flowers contain flavonoids and other antioxidants that help soothe the digestive system, providing relief for upset stomach.
Leaves: Rich in calcium and vitamin B, chrysanthemum leaves can help regulate stomach acid levels, alleviating discomfort caused by acid reflux or indigestion.
Rhyzomes: Packed with essential oils and alkaloids, chrysanthemum rhyzomes promote the secretion of digestive enzymes, aiding in proper nutrient absorption and reducing symptoms of upset stomach.
Buds: The potent anti-inflammatory properties of chrysanthemum buds make them an ideal addition to medicinal tea, providing relief for inflammation-related stomach ailments.
Seeds: A natural source of dietary fiber, chrysanthemum seeds encourage regular bowel movements, easing the symptoms of upset stomach and promoting overall digestive health.

teas.tea_desc: Lemon balm tea brings relief to an upset stomach due to its antispasmodic properties, which relax the gastrointestinal muscles and alleviate discomfort. This soothing effects on the digestive system stems from citral and citronellal, active compounds in lemon balm known to reduce inflammation. Moreover, lemon balm encourages the production of bile in the liver, promoting healthy digestion and easing symptoms of indigestion. As a natural carminative, it effectively dispels gas from the intestines, diminishing bloating and pain. Ultimately, lemon balm tea's calming effects on both body and mind contribute to overall stomach comfort, making it a gentle and reliable herbal remedy.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of lemon balm are rich in citronellal and other volatile oils, giving it a soothing effect on the digestive system, making it ideal for treating upset stomach.
Flowers: The small, white to pale purple flowers of lemon balm contain flavonoids that have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, aiding in relieving symptoms of upset stomach.
Stems: Although less potent than the leaves and flowers, the stems of lemon balm still contain therapeutic compounds. They contribute to a milder tea, soothing an upset stomach.
Seeds: The seeds of lemon balm have essential oils that promote digestion, making them useful in medicinal tea beneficial for upset stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Dill tea offers reliable relief for upset stomachs due to its carminative properties, which derive from certain volatile oils like limonene and carvone. These oils can quickly neutralize abdominal discomfort by encouraging the expulsion of intestinal gas, thereby alleviating bloating and cramps. Moreover, dill's antimicrobial qualities, stemming from compounds such as monoterpenes and flavonoids, help combat stomach infections caused by bacteria like E. coli, further diminishing digestive woes. Additionally, dill activates the secretion of bile from the liver, supporting healthy fat digestion and absorption. Lastly, this soothing tea exhibits anxiolytic effects, reducing stress and promoting relaxation, both of which contribute to overall gastrointestinal well-being.
tea_parts: Seeds: Dill seeds contain carminative properties that aid in reducing stomach pain and discomfort.
Leaves: Dill leaves have antispasmodic qualities that help soothe an upset stomach and promote healthy digestion.
Flowers: Dill flowers contain volatile oils which possess calming effects that can alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Coriander tea eases upset stomachs due to its carminative properties, which enable it to reduce gas and bloating. This tea's antidiarrheal qualities, derived from its tannins and essential oils, help solidify stools and reduce the frequency of bowel movements. Furthermore, coriander tea's antimicrobial and antispasmodic effects fight infections while soothing the digestive tract's muscles, diminishing discomfort. Its anti-inflammatory properties, attributed to its richness in cineole, linoleic acid, and other volatile acids, alleviate inflammation in the stomach lining. Lastly, coriander tea's significant potassium content replenishes electrolytes, promoting overall digestive health.
tea_parts: Seeds: Coriander seeds are rich in essential oils and compounds like linalool and borneol that aid in soothing digestive issues, making them ideal for medicinal tea.
Leaves: Coriander leaves, also known as cilantro, have antispasmodic and carminative properties that relieve stomach cramps and gas, making them a good choice for medicinal tea.
Stems: Coriander stems contain similar digestive benefits as the leaves, and they add a mild flavor and texture to medicinal tea, soothing an upset stomach.

Rhizomes: Coriander rhizomes, though less commonly used, have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties that soothe the digestive system and alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach, making them suitable for medicinal tea.
